column1: **We propose the following definition** of peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM): It is an idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure secondary to left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction

column2: the most recent international consensus definition of peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is given in the 2018 European Society of Cardiology (ESC) Guidelines for the Management of Cardiovascular Diseases during Pregnancy (Regitz2018 pages 34-34). This document
column3: a formal guideline from a major international cardiology society
column4: represents the most current authoritative position. It defines PPCM as “an idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure secondary to left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction
column5: with an LV ejection fraction (LVEF) usually <45%
column6: occurring towards the end of pregnancy or in the months following delivery.” It further notes that the LV “may be non‑dilated” and that “diagnosis requires excluding other causes of heart failure” (Regitz2018 pages 34-34). This definition is consonant with earlier position statements from the Heart Failure Association of the ESC Working Group on PPCM (2010)
column7: which describe PPCM as an idiopathic cardiomyopathy with LV systolic dysfunction and LVEF nearly always <45%
column8: presenting toward the end of pregnancy or in the months after delivery
column9: and being a diagnosis of exclusion (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4
column10: Sliwa2010a pages 2-2). The 2010 statements employ the language “we propose” and “simplified definition
column11: ” but these are standard rhetorical conventions in position papers and represent the formal consensus of the working group. Indeed
column12: the 2018 ESC guideline adopts essentially the same criteria
column13: confirming that the core definition has remained stable and is the accepted international standard. Notably
column16: and Blood Institute (NHLBI) workshop offered a slightly narrower temporal window—development during the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months of delivery—but the 2010 and 2018 definitions have broadened this to the “end of pregnancy” and “months following delivery” to avoid underdiagnosis (Sliwa2010a pages 2-2

column1: The question of how de novo PPCM is differentiated from underlying chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) that predates pregnancy is not explicitly addressed in the 2018 ESC guideline itself
column2: but it is extensively discussed in the 2010 position statement and the NHLBI workshop notes
column3: which together provide the conceptual framework for this differentiation. First and foremost
column4: the definition of PPCM as a “diagnosis of exclusion” (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4
column5: Sliwa2010a pages 2-2) inherently requires that pre‑existing heart disease be ruled out. The 2010 position statement explicitly states that this requirement “implies that pre-existing heart disease must be excluded
column6: thereby differentiating de novo PPCM from underlying chronic HFrEF that predates pregnancy” [Reference not found in provided documents].

column1: Several clinical features are cited to aid this distinction:
1. **Timing of presentation.** PPCM most commonly presents in the post‑partum period
column2: whereas idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (IDCM) that is unmasked by pregnancy typically becomes clinically evident by the second trimester. The context notes: “PPCM most commonly presents post‑partum
column3: whereas IDC presents by the 2nd trimester” [Reference not found in provided documents]. This temporal divergence is a key differentiating factor. Additionally
column4: the NHLBI-derived concept emphasizes that PPCM is not merely an aggravation of underlying IDCM by the volume overload of pregnancy
column5: because it often presents after delivery
column6: when the volume load has already diminished [Reference not found in provided documents]. This pathophysiological reasoning supports the view that PPCM is a distinct entity rather than a pregnancy‑exacerbated chronic cardiomyopathy.

column1: 2. **Cardiac dimensions.** The 2010 position statement notes that IDCM “usually has larger cardiac dimensions” compared with PPCM [Reference not found in provided documents]. While PPCM may present with a non‑dilated left ventricle (Regitz2018 pages 34-34
column3: chronic HFrEF typically is associated with progressive LV dilatation. A smaller ventricular size or absence of dilatation therefore argues for de novo PPCM.

column1: 3. **Association with pre‑eclampsia.** The context acknowledges that diagnostic confusion can arise when cardiac changes accompany pre‑eclampsia. Studies with a higher proportion of pre‑eclampsia patients report a greater frequency of PPCM presenting in the last month of pregnancy
column2: whereas studies that minimize pre‑eclampsia show a post‑partum peak 2–62 days after delivery [Reference not found in provided documents]. This observation underscores that the clinical profile of PPCM can be modulated by comorbid conditions
column3: but the essential distinction from pre‑existing HFrEF remains the absence of prior cardiac dysfunction.

column1: 4. **Exclusion of other causes.** The diagnosis of PPCM requires a thorough work‑up to exclude alternative etiologies such as valvular heart disease
column5: Sliwa2010a pages 3-4). The absence of prior documentation of LV systolic dysfunction

column2: the most recent international consensus (2018 ESC guideline) provides a precise definition of PPCM that is fully consistent with earlier position statements. The differentiation between de novo PPCM and antecedent chronic HFrEF is not reiterated in the 2018 guideline but is well elaborated in the 2010 working group position statement and the NHLBI workshop. The differentiation relies on: (a) the requirement that no other cause of heart failure be found (diagnosis of exclusion); (b) the typical timing of PPCM presentation in the post‑partum period
column3: as opposed to the second‑trimester onset of IDCM; (c) smaller or non‑dilated LV dimensions in PPCM compared with IDCM; and (d) the concept that PPCM is not simply an aggravation of pre‑existing IDCM by pregnancy volume overload
column4: because it often occurs after delivery when volume load is reduced. Together
column5: these criteria enable clinicians to distinguish de novo PPCM from chronic HFrEF that predates pregnancy
column6: ensuring accurate diagnosis and appropriate management."
